Facts.text: Circus Maximus in Rome catches fire
Start of Dionysian Pascal Cycle
Muslim forces under Tariq ibn Ziyad defeat the Visigoths led by their king Roderic
Battle at Alarcos: Almohads led by Abu Yusuf Yaqub al-Mansur defeats army of Alfonso VIII of Castile
Thomas of Buckingham's invasion army lands on Calais
38 Jews are burned at stake in Berlin, Prussia
Peasants' War begins in Germany's Black Forest
Catholic German monarchy form Union of Dessau
Italian War of 1542: The Siege of Boulogne begins
Treaty of Karlsburg: Arch Duke Ferdinand of Austria recognized as King of Hungary and Transylvania
Battle at Saint Ghislain: Spanish army beats The Genlis' mercenaries
Spanish viceroy Gilles of Hierges attacks Oudewater
Jacob Corneliszoon van Necks' Dutch merchant fleet, the second to reach Indonesia, leaves Java
French troops occupy Salses, at Perpignan
Soldiers killed governor of Aerssen in Paramaribo
The formal request to found the later city of Mayagüez, Puerto Rico is filed by its founders
Survivors of French frigate Medusa rescued off Senegal after 17 days
Fire in NYC destroys 1,000 homes and kills many
German Parliament demands Dutch province of Limburg
First railroad reaches Kansas
Battle of Buffington's Island (St George Creek), Ohio
Third Battle of Winchester, Virginia (Stephenson's Depot) [->JUL 20]
Dutch Red Cross forms
France declares war on Prussia; Franco-Prussian war begins
Emma Abbott, a floating hospital for sick children, makes trial trip, NYC
1st Wimbledon Men's Tennis: 27-year-old English rackets player Spencer Gore wins inaugural event; beats William Marshall 6-1, 6-2, 6-4
Russo-Turkish War: First Russian assault on Plevna, Ottoman Empire (now Pleven, Bulgaria) fails; 3,000 Russian and 2,000 Turk casualties
San Francisco Public Library starts lending books
National Brotherhood of Electrical Workers forms
Luxembourg born Michel Théato representing France wins Paris Olympics marathon in 2:59:45 ahead of another French athlete Émile Champion
1st Tour de France: French rider Maurice Garin wins inaugural event
Dutch Premier Kuypers disbands 1st Chamber
Llamas war party attacks group of 60, fleeing French mission at Tzekou, (Chinese-Tibetan border) murdering most with Scottish botanist George Forrest escaping by hiding for nine days
Under pressure from the Japanese, the Emperor of Korea abdicates in favor of his son, a figurehead
Dutch football club Feyenoord is established in Rotterdam as Wilhelmina; reverts to SC Feijenoord 1912; updated to SC Feyenoord 1974 and to Feyenoord Rotterdam 1978
Cleveland shortstop Neal Ball completes the first modern MLB unassisted triple play in the Indians' 6-1 win over the Boston Red Sox
A meteorite of estimated 190kg mass explodes over Holbrook in Navajo County, Arizona, causing approximately 16,000 pieces of debris to rain down on the town

Billboard publishes its earliest known chart, "Last Week's 10 Best Sellers Among Popular Songs." The #1 song is "Malinda's Wedding Day" by singers Byron Harlan and Arthur Collins, recorded in Camden, New Jersey.
Boston Braves begin drive from last to the NL pennant with a 3-2 win against the 2nd placed Cincinnati Reds
Dutch accidents at sea law enforced
The Washington Senators set a MLB record 8 stolen bases in an innings (1st) in 11-4 win v Cleveland

Washington Senators catcher Eddie Ainsmith applies for draft deferment; Secretary of War, Newton D Baker rules baseball players not draft exempt but later moves date to 1 September; both leagues end regular season 2 September
World War I: German armies retreat across Marne River in France
Following Peace Day celebrations marking the end of World War I, ex-servicemen rioted and burnt down Luton Town Hall.
WRC-AM in Washington, D.C. begins radio transmissions
King Fuad of Egypt seizes power and disbands parliament
Spanish premier Casares Quiroga succeeded by Jose Giral
Entartete Art Fair opens in Munich
Joris Ivens' "Spanish Earth" premieres in Hollywood
Dr Roy P Scholz is 1st surgeon to use fiberglass sutures
Nazi occupiers imprison 231 prominent Dutch citizens in Buchenwald
Nazi occupiers in Netherlands forbid anti-nazi films
First US Army flying school for black cadets dedicated in Tuskegee, Alabama
German occupiers confiscate bicycles in Rotterdam and The Hague
Transport #7 departs Drancy, France with 999 French Jews sent to Auschwitz Concentration camp; 17 survive until the war's end
500 allied air forces raid Rome during WW II
1,200+ 8th US Air Force bombers bomb targets in SW Germany
500 15th US Air Force Liberators/Flying Fortresses bomb Munich vicinity
Allied troops occupy Faubourg de Vaucelles, at Caen
Claus von Stauffenberg visits RC church in Berlin-Dahlem
Danish resistance fails in assault on Taarbaek Fort near Copenhagen
Democratic convention opens in Chicago, goes on to nominate PresidentFranklin D. Roosevelt
Japanese aircraft carriers Taiho and Shokaku sink in Marianas
NY archbishop Spellman flies to Europe
US generalOmar Bradleyflies to England
USS Cod saves 51 sailors from Dutch sub in only sub-to-sub rescue
French government of Schuman resigns
Laos becomes associated state within French Union
French/Vietnamese offensive against Viet Minh
Freddie Trueman takes 8-31, India all out 58 at Old Trafford
India all out 82 in 2nd innings after making 52 earlier in the day
XV Summer Olympic Games open at the Helsingin Olympiastadion, Helsinki, Finland
KIMA TV channel 29 in Yakima, WA (CBS) begins broadcasting
WAKR (now WAKC) TV channel 23 in Akron, OH (ABC) begins broadcasting
Balclutha ties up at Pier 43 in San Francisco and becomes a floating museum

First performance of Gerald Finzi's Cello Concerto, his last major work; John Barbirolli leads the Hallé Orchestra, with soloist Christopher Bunting at Cheltenham Music Festival in Gloucestershire, England
Yarkon Water Project opens to supply water to the Negev Desert in Israel
US refuse to lend Egypt money to build a second Aswan Dam
1st rocket with nuclear warhead fired, Yucca Flat, Nevada
US performs nuclear Test at Nevada Test Site
Italian government of Tambroni resigns
1st in-flight movie shown on TWA
Hungarian communist party expels Rákosi & Gero
NASA civilian Test pilot Joe Walker in X-15 reaches 105 km
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Shooting begins on Star Trek 2nd pilot "Where No Man Has Gone Before"
France performs nuclear test at Fangataufa Island
Governor James Rhodes declares state of emergency in Cleveland (race riot)
First air-conditioned NYC subway car (R-38 on the F line)
Race riots in Durham, North Carolina
US launches Explorer 35 for lunar orbit (800/7400 km)
Sudan military coup under maj Hashem al-Atta, Numeiry flees
Cleveland Indians pitcher Dick Bosman no-hits Oakland A's, 4-0
Soviet spacecraft Soyuz 14 lands back on earth
New York Yankees catcher Thurman Munson's first-inning single and RBI are nullified because tar on his bat handle exceeds the 18-inch limit; Minnesota Twins win 2-1
British rock group Deep Purple disbands
Sagarmatha National Park in Nepal is created.
Floods in Johnstown, Pennsylvania, kill 76
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
New York Yankees start historic 14 game comeback with 2-0 win over the Minnesota Twins at Metropolitan Stadium in Bloomington, MN
2 supertankers collide off Tobago-260,000 tons of oil spill
Patricia Harris becomes sect of HEW
USSR's Walter Polovchak is placed in US custody at 12 after his asylum application
XXII Summer Olympic Games open in Moscow, Russia; led by United States, 66 nations boycott event because of Soviet-Afghan war
South African Springbok rugby team arrives in New Zealand beginning 56 days of 'near civil war' in the country between anti-apartheid protesters and tour supporters
Bolivian government resigns
David S Dodge becomes 1st American hostage in Lebanon
The first three-dimensional reconstruction of a human head in a CT is published
1st female to captain a 747 across Atlantic (Lynn Rippelmeyer)
Christa McAuliffe chosen as first school teacher to fly aboard the space shuttle
The Val di Stava Dam collapse killing 268 people in Val di Stava, Italy
NFL owners vote unanimously to form World League of American Football - operated 1991-2007
United Airlines DC-10 crashes at Sioux City Iowa, kills 112
BASF plant in Cincinnati explodes in flames, 1 dies
Richard Nixon library opens in Yorba Linda, California
With NY Yankee victory, 10 of 14 AL teams are at .500 or better
Ebony P Warren crowned 24th Miss Black America
NYC ballet dancer Peter Martins is arrested for beating his wife
Glen Chapple (Lancs) scores fastest F-C century, 21 minutes
Bomb explosion in Alas Airlines at Colon Panama, killing 21
First game ever canceled at Seattle Kingdome due to falling tiles
Leonid Kvetjsma sworn in as Ukraine president
Jason Gallian scores 312 in 683 mins for Lancashire v Derbyshire
XXVI Summer Olympic Games open in Atlanta, Georgia
Goodwill Games IV opens in New York City
Michael Brunet discovers the skull of Sahelanthropus tchadensis in the Djurab Desert, Chad, one of the oldest known species in the human family tree and 6-7 million years old
The Wesley Impact: black spot the size of Earth is discovered on Jupiter after unknown object crashes onto the planet
20 people are killed by a suicide bombing in Diyala, Iraq
World Health Organization puts world's Ebola death toll at 11,284
Archaeological dig in Kakadu National Park extends Aboriginal peoples time in Australia to 65,000 to 80,000 years ago
BBC publishes salaries of its top-earning journalists and presenters, two-thirds male, Chris Evans highest at over £2.2 million
US scientists calculate the total amount of plastic ever produced: 8.3 billion tonnes, equal to the weight of 1 billion elephants
A duck boat sinks in Table Rock Lake, Missouri, drowning 17 people
Brazilian soccer international goalkeeper Alisson transfers from Roma to Liverpool for a world record shot blocker's fee of £66.8m
First commercial flight, the "bird of peace" between Ethiopia and Eritrea in 20 years lands in Asmara, Eritrea, reuniting families
Israel's parliament passes controversial "nation state" law giving only Jews self-determination, relegating Arabic to "special status"
Largest intact sarcophagus of its kind ever found (2000 years old) opened in Alexandria, contains 3 skeletons, not a curse as feared
Russian ship Dmitri Donskoii, with possible cargo of gold coins is discovered in waters between South Korea and Japan where it sunk in 1905
Heat wave begins across the east of America affecting 100 million people and killing 6, with New York city declaring a state of emergency
Largest wind farm in Africa opens at Lake Turkana, Kenya, generating 310 megawatts
UK oil tanker Stena Impero seized by Iranian Revolutionary Guard in the Strait of Hormuz
First Arab interplanetary mission, the Mars "Hope" orbiter launches on board Japanese H-2A rocket from Tanegashima Space Center
Portland Mayor Ted Wheeler criticizes federal policing of protesters in his city, calling it unconstitutional
Bomb attack on al-Wuhailat market in Baghdad, Iraq, ahead of Eid al-Adha festival, kills 25 people
Peruvian union leader Pedro Castillo declared winner of the country's presidential election (11 April), defeating Keiko Fujimori by only 44,000 votes
UK lifts most COVID-19 restrictions on so-called "Freedom Day" despite 50,000 new daily infections
Temperatures exceed 40°C in parts of England for first time
Massive landslide after heavy monsoon rains in India leaves at least 27 dead in Irshalwadi village near Mumbai, with 78 still missing
Scientists discover metals can self-heal after observing metals cracking and fusing back together, paving the way for future self-healing structures and vehicles
At least 15 people die when a bridge collapses in Shangluo in China’s Shaanxi province after heavy rains and flash flooding
Missouri woman Sandra Hemme released from prison after her 43 year conviction for murder was overturned - longest known wrongful conviction of a woman in US history
Thirteen sharpnose sharks are the first to test positive for cocaine off the coast of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
Widespread technology outage grounds flights and shuts down bank and media companies around the world, caused by an update error by cyber security firm CrowdStrike
Mansa Musa, ruler of the Mali Empire, arrives in Cairo on his way to Mecca, with a procession of 600,000 men, 12,000 slaves and 80 camels carrying 136 kg (300 pounds) of gold each
Duke John VI of Brabant pledges Holland/Zealand toPhilip the Good
KingHenry VIII's flagship Mary Rose sinks at Portsmouth; 73 die
15-year-old LadyJane Greydeposed as England's Queen after 9 days
Albert Frederickfollows his father becoming Duke of Prussia after a symbolic ceremony of feudal homage to Polish King Sigismund Augustus
KingPhilip II's powerful secretary Antonio Pérez escapes jail in Madrid and flees to Aragon
AstronomerJohannes Keplerhas an epiphany and develops his theory of the geometrical basis of the universe while teaching in Graz, Austria
Court of Holland bansbooks of Hobbes, Spinoza and Meyer
Russian TsarPeter I's fleet occupies the southern mouth of the Don River and gains access to the Sea of Azov, after the surrender of the Ottoman Azov garrison
Great Northern War: Swedish troops under KingCharles XIIoccupy Kraków, Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth
HMS Beagle withCharles Darwinarrives in Ascension Island
American cargo ship 'Elizabeth', returning from Italy, wrecks in a storm of coast of Fire Island, New York, killing 10, including journalistMargaret Fuller, her husband, and child
US Congress passes 3rd Reconstruction Act over PresidentAndrew Johnson's veto
Louis Rielspeaks at a meeting of Metis residents about rights, setting in motion the events now referred to as the Red River Rebellion
Sioux Indian ChiefSitting Bullsurrenders to US federal troops at Fort Buford in the Territory of Montana
Cy Youngwins the 500th game of his Baseball HOF career as the Cleveland Naps beat Washington Senators, 5 - 2, in 11 innings; only pitcher in MLB history to reach milestone
Babe Ruthhits his 30th home run of the 1920 baseball season, breaking his own single-season record. He would attain 54 throughout the season, before breaking that record the next year.
American swimmerJohnny Weissmullerfirst to break 1 minute barrier for 100m freestyle; swims 58.6s at Alameda, CA
Second French government ofÉdouard Herriotforms
Richard E. Byrd, Laurence McKinley Gould, and their polar expedition team return to the United States following the first exploration of the interior of Antarctica
Rick Ferrell(Red Sox) andWes Ferrell(Cleveland Indians), two brothers on opposite teams, hit home runs in the same game, a first in MLB history
17-year-old Cleveland Indians future Baseball HOF pitcherBob Fellermakes his MLB debut in relief in 9-5 loss to Senators at Griffith Stadium, Washington
Adolf Hitlerorders Great Britain to surrender - they decline
BBC World Service begins playing V(ictory) ("...-" in Morse code) (opening ofLudwig van Beethoven's 5th symphony)
British PMWinston Churchilllaunches his "V for Victory" campaign
Tom and Jerry first appear under their own names in the cartoon "The Midnight Snack" byWilliam HannaandJoseph Barbera
US PresidentFranklin D. Rooseveltappoints the Fair Employment Practice Committee (FEPC)
US première ofDmitri Shostakovich's 7th Symphony in NYC by the NBC Symphony Orchestra underArturo Toscaniniin a concert broadcast nationwide on NBC radio
Swedish diplomat Raoul Wallenberg 1st meetsAdolf Eichmann
Prime Minister of shadow Burma government, BogyokeAung Sanand 6 of his cabinet and 2 non-cabinet members assassinated by armed paramilitaries
NY Yankees sign their 1st Black players,Elston Howardand Frank Baines
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Summi maeroris
Elvis Presley's debut single, a cover of Arthur Cruddup's "That's All Right" is released
NY Mets future Baseball Hall of Fame outfielderWillie Maysis named to NL All Star team for 24th time (ties Stan Musial)
NASA's Apollo 18 and SovietSoyuz 19linked in orbit for 2 days, separate
Allman Brother's roadie Scooter Herring sentenced to 75 years for providing drugs for the group, based onGregg Allman's testimony
Nicaragua Liberation Day; Sandinistas take over fromAnastasio Somoza Debayle

1st annual Cracker Jack Old-Timers Baseball Classic: 75-year-old Luke Appling hits a 275-foot HR offWarren Spahn, as AL beats NL 7-2; participants includedHank Aaron,Lou Brock,Bert Campaneris,Joe DiMaggio,Stan Musial, Don Newcombe, andEarly Wynn(RFK Stadium, Washington, D.C.)
Geraldine Ferraro(US Representative, New York), becomes the 1st US female major-party vice-presidential nominee, officially joiningWalter Mondale's Democratic Party ticket
Tim Witherspoon KOsFrank Brunoin 11 for heavyweight boxing title
New York YankeesDon Mattinglysets AL record of extra base hits in 10 consecutive games
Cleveland IndiansJoe Carterhas his 4th 3 HR game
Dave Righettipitches in his 499th game as a NY Yankee, passing Whitey Ford in most appearances as a NY Yankee
Former MLB baseball player and managerPete Roseis sentence to 5 months for tax evasion
Cal Ripkenplays in his 1,500th consecutive game
Mike Tysonrapes a Miss Black America contestant (Desiree Washington)
President Clintonfires FBI director William Sessions
Teen comedy film "Clueless" is released, loosely based onJane Austen's book "Emma" starringAlicia Silverstone, directed by Amy Heckerling
TV drama "Mad Men" starringJon Hamm,Elisabeth Moss,January Jones, and Vincent Kartheiser, debuts on cable network AMC
17th ESPY Awards:Michael Phelps, Nastia Liukin win
"Captain America: The First Avenger", directed by Joe Johnston, starringChris Evansand Hayley Atwell, premieres in Los Angeles, California
Selena Gomezreleases her debut solo studio album "Stars Dance"
"Despacito" by Luis Fonsi featuringDaddy Yankeebecomes the most streamed song ever, played 4.6 billion times
Investigators using DNA identify 16-year-old James Byron Haakenson as a victim of serial killerJohn Wayne Gacy, 41 years after his disappearance
US SenatorJohn McCaindiagnosed with brain cancer
Iran’s PresidentHassan Rouhanisays 25 million Iranians already infected with COVID-19, 35 million at risk (official figure just 269,440)
World Formula 1 drivers championLewis Hamiltonwins a record 8th Hungarian Grand Prix in Budapest to equal Michael Schumacher's record of wins at a single circuit (French GP)

Five more people - Rebecca Nurse, Susannah Martin, Elizabeth How, Sarah Good, and Sarah Wildes - hanged for allegedly practicing witchcraft as a result of the Salem witch trials in Massachusetts Bay Colony
Steamship SS Great Britain is launched, designed byIsambard Kingdom Brunel, it is the first ocean-going craft with an iron hull or screw propeller and the largest vessel afloat in the world
First US women's rights convention is held in Seneca Falls, NY, organized byElizabeth Cady Stantonand Lucretia Mott
Apollo 11 goes into lunar orbit
Airbus Beluga XL, painted to look like the whale, makes its first flight, landing in Toulouse-Blagnac, France
Erhard Buttner, German organist and composer, born in Römhild, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1625)
Richard Leveridge, English bass singer and composer, born in St Martin-in-the-Fields, London (d. 1758)
Giuseppe Castiglione, Italian Jesuit brother and missionary to China, born in Milan, Italy (d. 1766)
Garret Wesley, 1st Earl of Mornington, Anglo-Irish composer, born in Dangan Castle, County Meath, Ireland (d. 1781)
Jean-Baptiste Davaux, French classical composer, born in La Côte-Saint-André, France (d. 1822)
Heinrich Christian Boie, German author and editor, born in Meldorf, Germany (d. 1806)
Alessio Prati, Italian composer, born in Ferrara, Papal States (d. 1788)
Seraphim of Sarov, Russian Orthodox Saint, born in Kursk, Russian Empire (d. 1833) [OS]
Jonathan Blewitt, English organist and composer, born in London (d. 1853)
John Martin, English painter and engraver, born in Haydon Bridge, Northumberland, England (d. 1854)
Johann Gottlieb Schneider, German composer, born in Neugersdorf, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1856)
Juan José Flores, General and first President of Ecuador (1839-1845), born in Puerto Cabello, Venezuela (d. 1864)
Vincenz Lachner, German composer, born in Rain am Lech, Kingdom of Bavaria (d.. 1893)
Mary Ann Bickerdyke, American army nurse (union), born in Knox County, Ohio (d. 1901)
Gottfried Keller, German-Swiss poet and novelist, born in Zurich (d. 1890)
Princess Augusta of Cambridge, Grand Duchess of Mecklenburg-Strelitz and granddaughter of George III, born in Palace of Montbrillant, Hanover (d. 1916)
George Henry Gordon, American lawyer and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Charlestown, Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1886)
Mangal Pandey, Indian Sepoy (soldier) in the 34th Bengal Native Infantry (B.N.I.) regiment of the British East India Company, born in Nagwa, Ballia district, India (d. 1857)
Wilhelm Kalliwoda, Bohemian-German pianist, composer, kapellmeister, and music teacher, born in Donaueschingen, Grand Duchy of Baden (d. 1893)
Roger Atkinson Pryor, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Petersburg, Virginia (d. 1919)
John Wesley Turner, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Saratoga, New York (d. 1899)
Joel Asaph Allen, American zoologist (Allen's rule) and first curator of birds and mammals at the American Museum of Natural History, born in Springfield, Massachusetts (d. 1921)
Frederic T. Greenhalge, English-born American lawyer and 38th Governor of Massachusetts, born in Clitheroe, England (d. 1896)
Cornelis Adrianus Pekelharing, Dutch physician, physiologist, and academic, born in Zaandam, Netherlands (d. 1922)
Ferdinand Brunetière, French writer and critic, born in Toulon, Var, France (d. 1906)
François Victor Alphonse Aulard, French historian, born in Montbron, France (d. 1928)
Hendrik Jut, Dutch murderer (Head of Jut), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1878)
Daniel Josephus Jitta, Dutch High Court lawyer, born in Amsterdam (d. 1925)
Emil Paur, Austrian conductor (Boston Symphony; New York Philharmonic; Pittsburgh Symphony; Berlin State Opera), born in Czernowitz, Austria (d. 1932)
Dwijendralal Ray, Bengali poet, playwright and lyricist (Dwijendrageeti), born in Krishnanagar, British India (d. 1913)
Hermann Bahr, Austrian writer, editor, dramaturg, and playwright (The Concert), and literary critic, born in Linz, Austria (d. 1934)
Charles Horace Mayo, American surgeon and co-founder (Mayo Clinic), born in Rochester, Minnesota (d. 1939)
Louis Zimmermann, Dutch violinist (1st concert master of Amsterdam Symphony), composer, and educator, born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1954)
John Gunn, English cricket medium pace bowler (6 Tests, 18 wickets, BB 5/76; Nottinghamshire CCC), born in Hucknall, England (d. 1963)
Joseph Fielding Smith Jr., 10th president of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 1972)
Arthur Fielder, English cricket fast bowler (6 Tests 1903-08), born in Plaxtol, Tonbridge, Kent (d. 1949)
Robert Herman Woltjer, Dutch academic and politician (Member of  Senate, 1937-52; Minister of Education), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1955)
Hendrik Gerrit Cannegieter, meteorology/director (KNMI), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1964)
Friedrich Dessauer, German physicist (radiologist), born in Aschaffenburg, Germany (d. 1963)
Max Fleischer, Polish-American animator and film producer (Betty Boop, Popeye the Sailor Man), born in Kraków, Austrian Poland (d. 1972)
Eugène Jungers, Belgian Governor of Rwanda Urundi, born in Messancy, Belgium (d. 1958)
George II, King of Greece (1922-24 and 1935-47), born at Tatoi Palace in Parnitha, Kingdom of Greece (d. 1947)
Dick Irvin, Canadian Hockey HOF center (Chicago Black Hawks) and coach (Stanley Cup 1932 Toronto Maple Leafs; 1944, 46, 53 Montreal Canadiens), born in Hamilton, Ontario, Canada (d. 1957)
Aleksandr Yakovlevich Khinchin, Russian mathematician, born in Kondrovo, Kaluga Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1959)
Joris van Severen, Flemish politician and founder of the Alliance of Dietsche National Solidarists, born in Wakken, Belgium (d. 1940)
Khawaja Nazimuddin, 2nd Prime Minister of Pakistan and President of Pakistan Muslim League, born in Dacca, Bengal, British India (d. 1964)
Percy Spencer, American physicist and inventor of the microwave, born in Howland, Maine (d. 1970)[1]
Xu Beihong, Chinese painter, born in Yixing, China (d. 1953)
A. J. Cronin, Scottish writer (Citadel, Shining Victory), born in Cardross (d. 1981)
Juano Hernandez, Puerto Rican stage and film actor (St Louis Blues; Stars In My Crown), born in San Juan, Puerto Rico (d. 1970)
Juan Bautista Plaza, Venezuelan composer, born in Caracas, Venezuela (d. 1965)
Paul de Groot, Dutch communist politician (CPN) and editor (The Truth), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1986)
Arno Breker, German artist and "official state sculptor" of Nazi Germany (Die Partei), born in Elberfeld, German Empire (d. 1991)
Marc Turfkruijer, Flemish football referee, movie journalist, and writer, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1990)
Roscoe [Charles Barnet] Harvey, British soldier, born in Sarawak, Malaysian Borneo (d. 1996)
(William) "Buster" Bailey, American jazz clarinetist (WC Handy; King Oliver's Creole Jazz Band; Fletcher Henderson; Noble Sissle; John Kirby), and bandleader, born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 1967)
Gerard Rutten, Dutch film director (Miraculous Life of Willem Parel), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1982)
Boyd Neel, English conductor (Story of an Orchestra), born in Blackheath, London (d. 1981)
Edgar Snow, American author and journalist (Red star over China), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1972)
Louis Kentner, Hungarian-British pianist and composer, born in Karviná, Austrian Silesia (d. 1987)
Klaus Egge, Norwegian composer (Noregsonger), born in Gransherad, Telemark, Norway (d. 1979)
Wim van Doorne, Dutch auto manufacturer (DAF Trucks), born in Griendtsveen, Limburg, Netherlands (d. 1978)
Günter Bialas, German composer and music educator, born in Bielschowitz, Prussian Silesia (now Bielszowice, Poland) (d. 1995)
Isabel Jewell, American actress (Gone With The Wind), born in Shoshoni, Wyoming (d. 1972)
Jeno Vecsey, Hungarian composer, born in Cece, Hungary (d. 1966)
Norman Carr, British conservationist working in Central and Southern Africa, born in Chinde, Portuguese East Africa (d. 1997)
Josef Páleníček, Czech composer, born in Travnik, Bosnia and Herzegovina (d. 1991)
Marius Russo, American baseball pitcher (MLB All-Star 1941; World Series 1941, 43; New York Yankees), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2005)
James D. Ramage, American Naval Aviator in World War II, the Korean War, the Vietnam War and the Cold War, born in Waterloo, Iowa (d. 2012)
Phil Cavarretta, American baseball utility (MLB All-Star 1944, 45, 46, 47; NL MVP 1945; Chicago Cubs), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2010)
Robert Aitken, Zen co-founder (Diamond Sangha), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2010)
William Scranton, American Republican Party politician and diplomat, born in Madison, Connecticut (d. 2013)
Patricia Medina, British actress (The Three Musketeers, High-Low), born in Liverpool, England (d. 2012)
Robert Pinget, French novelist (The Interrogation) and playwright, born in Geneva (d. 1997)
Robert Christiani, West Indian cricket batsman (22 Tests, 1 x 100, 4 x 50, HS 107; British Guiana), born in Georgetown, British Guiana (d. 2005)
Robert Mann, American composer and violinist (String Quartet 1952), born in Portland, Oregon (d. 2018)
Rosalyn Yalow, American medical physicist (Nobel 1977), born in New York City (d. 2011)
Al Haig, American bebop jazz pianist (Charlie Parker; Stan Getz; Chet Baker), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 1982)
Harold Camping, American evangelist and founder of Family Radio, born in Boulder, Colorado (d. 2013)
Rachel Robinson, American social activist and humanitarian (The Jackie Robinson Foundation), born in Los Angeles, California

Alex Hannum, American Basketball HOF coach (1st ABA, NBA winning coach; ABA C'ship 1969 Oakland Oaks; NBA C'ship 1958 St. Louis Hawks, 1967 Philadelphia 76ers), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2002)
David Allyn [Albert DiLello], American jazz singer (Boyd Raeburn Band), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 2012)[1]
William A. Rusher, American lawyer and columnist, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2011)

Arthur Rankin, Jr, American director, producer, and screenwriter (Rankin/Bass Productions -The Year Without A Santa Claus; Rudolph The Red-Nosed Reindeer; Frosty The Snowman), born in New York City (d. 2014)
Martin Patterson "Pat" Hingle, American actor (Hang 'em High, Splendor in the Grass, Batman Returns), born in Miami, Florida (d. 2009)
Stanley K. Hathaway, American politician (27th Governor of Wyoming), born in Osceola, Nebraska (d. 2005)
Vassar Miller, American poet (Wage War on Silence, Onions and Roses), born in Houston, Texas (d. 1998)
Jack Petchey English businessman, philanthropist and football executive (Chairman Watford FC 1987-94), born in Plaistow, England (d. 2024)
Sue Thompson [Eva Sue McKee], American pop and country music singer ("Sad Movies (Make Me Cry")), born in Nevada, Missouri (d. 2021)
Helen Gallagher, American Emmy and Tony Award-winning singer, dancer, and actress (No, No, Nanette; Ryan's Hope - "Maeve"), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2024)

Billy Gardner, American baseball manager (Minnesota Twins 1981–85, 
KC Royals 1987) and second baseman (World Series 1954 NY Giants, 1961 Minnesota Twins), born in Waterford, Connecticut (d. 2024)
Jan Myrdal, Swedish writer and journalist (Albania Defiant), born in Bromma, Stockholm (d. 2020)
John Bratby, English painter and writer (Breakdown), born in Wimbledon, London (d. 1992)
Arley "Buster" Benton, American blues singer and guitarist, born in Texarkana, Arkansas (d. 1996)
Bobby Bradford, American jazz trumpeter (John Carter; Ornette Coleman; The Mo'tet), bandleader, composer, and educator (Pomona College, 1977-2021), born in Cleveland, Mississippi
Francisco Sá Carneiro, Portuguese politician (founder of Social Democratic Party; 109th Prime Minister of Portugal, 1980), born in Porto, Portugal (d. 1980)
Willem Nijholt, Dutch actor (De Nietsnut, Respect, Op Afbetaling), born in Gombong, Central Java (d. 2023)
